title: What Good Code Looks Like
intro: This function works perfectly -- it processes an order, sends an email, and updates the database. But it violates a core principle of clean code. Find the line where the problem starts.

teach.title: Good Code = Good Writing
teach.body: Good code is like good writing: each paragraph covers one topic, sentences are clear, and there's a logical flow. You don't need to be a genius to write good code, just like you don't need to be Shakespeare to write a clear email. The goal is that someone else (or future you) can read it without getting confused.

teach.title: The Single Responsibility Principle
teach.body:
The most important rule of clean code: **each function should do one thing**.

Ask yourself: 'Can I describe what this function does without using the word AND?' If not, it's doing too much.

- 'This function validates the form' -- good, one thing
- 'This function validates the form AND sends the email AND updates the database' -- bad, three things

teach.title: One Function, One Job
teach.annotation: The good version reads like a table of contents. You can understand the entire flow at a glance. If the email sending breaks, you know exactly where to look: sendConfirmationEmail().

teach.body: Good code has functions that do one thing, variables that describe their contents, and a structure you can navigate without a map. The single responsibility principle -- each function does one thing -- is the foundation of clean code.

exercise.prompt: This function validates items, calculates totals, sends emails, updates inventory, processes payment, creates an order, AND sends a confirmation -- all in one loop starting at line 3. Which line marks the start of the 'doing too much' problem?

fixes.text: // Add comments explaining each section of the loop
try { /* same code */ } catch(e) { /* handle error */ }

exercise.explanation: The loop starting at line 3 mixes validation, calculation, email sending, and database updates. If the payment fails on line 11, the inventory is already decremented and a notification email was already sent -- you can't undo those. The fix is splitting into separate functions: validate first, then calculate, then update inventory, then charge, then confirm. Option B (comments) doesn't fix the tangled logic. Option C (try-catch) doesn't separate the concerns. Option D changes loop syntax but not structure.
